column1: What is the candidate's name as it will appear on the ballot?
column2: Please describe any professional and/or life experiences that will help voters assess your qualifications for the office you’re seeking.
column3: Where do you live currently? Where (city and state) were you born? How long have you lived in Montana?
column4: Age as of Nov. 3, 2026, date of birth, and place of birth. We will not publish your birth date.
column5: What other biographical information do you want voters to know about you?
column6: What should be the PSC's role, if any, in regulating data center development in Montana?
column7: How should the PSC approach the NorthWestern/Black Hills Corp. merger?
column8: What is one industry under the PSC's purview that could use more scrutiny than it's currently getting, and what might that increased scrutiny look like?

column2: I'm a CPA/Accountant and the ratemaking process is very focused on Profit & Loss Statements and Balance Sheets. I'm well positioned to turn a sharp eye to the books while making rate price decisions. I've also been a Legislator for what will be 8 years at the end of my term this year.
column3: I live in Great Falls. I was born in Missoula. I've lived in Montana for 41 years (I'm 41).
column4: I'll be 42 on Nov 3, 2026. My DOB is 06/03/1984, and was born in Missoula, MT.
column5: I'm married to Hannah, our 21st anniversary is coming up in July. I have four kids, ages 18, 16, 14, and 5 months old. In my professional life I've been an accountant at a nonprofit, corporate healthcare, and run my own small business (tax prep). I've owned residential rentals and commercial property which inform my thinking on property taxes and utility costs like gas and electric.
column6: The PSC should work with poliymakers cooperatively to form laws that have data centers "bring their own generation" or pay for their own power distribution infrastructure. The point being that data centers should not be allowed to increase electric rates because of extra invested assets being put into the rate base, so if they will be a burden to other ratepayers, policies should control for that.
column7: The PSC should follow the law and perform due diligence to decide whether the merger is in the best interest of ratepayers. The Montana PSC is not the only state regulator looking at this and each of the state regulatory commissions should work to ensure the deal is best for most constiuents. If the deal makes sense and produces economies of scale, and has a high potential to meet capacity needs, then it could work out well for ratepayers.
column8: The energy industry does get a lot of attention, so second to that, the waste hauling business is important to keep a keen eye on as well. It is a large industry and competition is limited. Adequate service at reasonable prices would be my goal and focus when regulating this area under the PSC's purview.

column2:
My family has been involved in the telecom industry since the early days of the Bell System, shaping my understanding of regulated services and their impact on captive rate payers. I was a partner and later CEO of a small local internet company, navigating regulations for over five years. My experience spans phone service, fiber internet, train services, and electrical services, engaging with these systems throughout my life. My tech journey began early, training local architects on AutoCAD. I’m driven by curiosity about how systems interconnect and use my skills to analyze and optimize processes.
Growing up in Montana instilled in me a strong commitment to investing in our communities, viewing neighbors and towns as vital resources. I’ve served on boards like Queen City Ballet and Healthy Mothers Healthy Babies. Additionally, I founded the Happiness & Joy Foundation, a 501(c)3 nonprofit that generates significant revenue for Helena annually through the Montana Pride celebration, which I’ve directed for over a decade.
column3: I currently live in East Helena, Montana. I was born in Denver, CO, after my parents had to move there because the GOP in the 1970s managed to damage the economy to the point that jobs in Montana were impossible to find, but we moved back when I was 5 and I started kindergarten at Four Georgians Elementary in Helena the first year that school was open. I graduated from Capital High and then attended Montana State, and then moved to Portland, OR, Phoenix, AZ, Caribou, ME, Houston, TX, and Sacramento, CA, before deciding in 2009 that yes, Helena is my home, and I’ve been here ever since.
column4: Jan 8, 1973, so I’ll be, and am, 53.
column5:
Kevin is a skilled businessman known for identifying new economic opportunities. He significantly contributed to ATX’s growth from $5 million to $20 million through his marketing expertise. Building on this success, he launched two thriving small businesses. In 2010, he founded Studio 19, which managed live video production for Frontier Conference athletics statewide. By 2018, his reputation as a problem solver expanded his customer base, enabling him to launch AuxTS, a company offering high-touch I.T. support to small businesses.
Kevin’s background is quintessentially Montanan. He worked out of necessity, valuing character over politics, and formed lifelong friendships with those who held differing views. He was educated in the Helena Public School system, from kindergarten thru graduation, and attended Montana State, a public education he deeply cherishes, and has found community through theatre, comedy, and activism.
As a candidate, Kevin stands with hard-working Montanans who lack family trust funds. Though not wealthy, he possesses something invaluable: a lifetime of solidarity with working Montanans and decades of experience advocating for them in every possible way.
column6:
Datacenters as they are “planned” now are so egregiously destructive to the area they are in that they need to be stopped. The PSC has the authority to make sure that any infrastructure upgrades needed to service these monstrosities are paid for by the actual customer, not by the people of Montana. The increase in electricity demand can also be shunted to the customers making it rather than foisting that bill onto residential customers. The water use and pollution are not, in most cases, directly regulated by the PSC but that doesn’t mean that we can’t lean in and force discussions about what’s best for Montanans.
The current farce of just taking the AI companies’ talking points and saying “Oh, this will be fine” cannot continue. Throughout Montana’s history we see, time and again, that monied interests lie through their teeth to build something in Montana—from a mine that will destroy the land and water nearby, to land buys that block the public from public lands—it’s always greedy elites that are trying to sell the beauty of Montana and abscond with bags of cash. And the people of Montana are left to pay the piper, time and again. It has to stop, and the PSC can lead the way.
column7: That the result had better put the cost per kW back to a reasonable rate, which honestly should be at or below what was charged when deregulation was put through. The whole goal of deregulation was to reduce the costs for consumers, and that has never happened. It absolutely should. Given that every merger is billed as a cost-savings for the companies as they find ‘synergies’ and ‘remove redundancies’ and all those savings are passed on to the shareholders after a hefty chunk of change is pocketed by the executive suite, I’m not holding my breath that this is going to be a good thing for anyone. I intend to press them both on how they are going to fix their aged infrastructure, replace outdated equipment and rethink how the entire grid in Montana is working, and make sure that they are not just taking more money from the people of Montana because the past PSC has had the oversight skills of a blind lapdog. We cannot continue to allow the executives at these utilities to boost their own wealth to undreamt of heights while the rest of us struggle to afford to keep the lights on. It’s ridiculous and Montanans deserve better.
column8:
Trains. The state’s railroads are outdated and absurd. We should have mandated infrastructure upgrades for all industries the PSC oversees, but we haven’t. Railroads should have been modernizing for decades with modern tracks, better engines, smarter and more traffic-friendly schedules, improved relations with workers’ unions, and technological advancements. There’s no reason that Montana Avenue is constantly blocked by multiple trains at high-traffic times in Helena when a simple upgrade to smarter scheduling tools would allow BNSF to avoid these conflicts. Or they could invest money into their tracks and crossings so that they are bridged or tunneled away from traffic. It’s 2026, yet we still have ancient steel tracks instead of having upgrades that could include high-speed bullet trains for passenger rail, when Japan has had this for half a century. And yes, we could adapt it for all our uses including cargo. We’re not dumb, the elites are just greedy.
Additionally, water systems need to ensure they serve their ratepayers effectively, rather than just extracting money for their ownership.

column2: My best qualification is that I am in my fourth year of service doing the job of Public Service Commissioner honestly, conscientiously, and effectively. This position has a learning curve. I understand the responsibilities, policies, and procedures. I know the staff and work well with them. Currently I'm working on future projects to serve upcoming needs of constituents. My education level is doctorate. Decades of practicing medicine taught me listening skills, the ability to accurately analyze complex data, and draw reasoned conclusions and solve problems based on facts. As commissioner, I've extensively researched topics related to PSC industry knowledge. As promised in my first campaign, I share findings through educational presentations available to you on my website, anniebukacek.com. Hard work, honesty, and dedication sum up my life as a longtime grassroots advocate for Montanans. I made campaign promises and I kept them.  I will keep life-sustaining energy dependable at the best possible cost, based on balancing affordability and reliability. You'll get service from me without interruption, with the same dependability you want from water, power, transportation, trash, etc.
column3: I was born in Chicago Illinois and have lived in Montana since November 1991. I currently travel back and forth from Helena to Kalispell. I am registered to vote in Helena.
column4: I was born in Chicago Illinois, March 1, 1958 so am 68 years old and will be 68 at the time of the election
column5: I know the lives of my children and grandchildren, friends and neighbors, are impacted for the short and long haul by decisions I make at the PSC.
column6: The jurisdictional role the PSC has in data center development is scrutinizing the large load tariffs relevant to data centers. This will be scrutinized carefully by PSC staff and commissioners with an eye to ensure that residential and mom and pop businesses will not be saddled with any of the data center’s share of energy generation demand, transmission, integration and service costs, to protect current residential and business users from rate and reliability adverse events.
column7: The PSC will approach the merger as it approaches other contested issues that come before the agency. The PSC is currently collecting public comments and prehearing testimony from the parties involved. PSC staff and commissioners use this information to develop questions for the parties during the proposed hearing this May in order to make the evidentiary record as robust as possible. Thereafter, we review the evidence available to us. A staff memorandum and possibly recommendation will be written, and the commissioners will make a decision.
column8: The PSC does its best to scrutinize every company it is tasked with overseeing and every matter that comes before it.  The time we have to do this is usually restricted by law.  The amount of staff hours that can be spent on items is dictated by the staff hours budgeted for us by the Legislature.  We always do our best to prioritize as to the item, financial impact, and relative need.  Commissioners and staff are working for the taxpayer and do not accumulate the expertise that management and workers do in their regulated industries.  The PSC is one of Montana's smallest state agencies.  The PSC could use additional funding to hire industry-related and experienced forensic examiners.  But money is tight, taxes are already high, and we will continue to do the best we can with the resources allocated.
